summary: Gruber highlights the growing CPU performance gap between Apple's A-series chips and Qualcomm's Snapdragon processors used in Android phones. Even Apple's $400 iPhone SE outperforms the most expensive Android flagships, and the discontinued iPhone 8 still beats every Android phone in single-threaded benchmarks. He notes that Android-focused media largely downplays this disparity, suggesting the coverage would be very different if the tables were turned. The situation is historically unprecedented: the company with the best chips keeps them exclusive to its own devices, rather than selling them to the broader market.
key_points: Apple's A13 chip delivers roughly 1.5x the single-threaded performance of the Snapdragon 865 and 1.8x the Snapdragon 855, making even Apple's budget phone faster than any Android flagship
The discontinued iPhone 8 from 2017 still has better single-threaded performance than any Android phone available in 2020
A $400-500 Android phone like the Moto Z4 benchmarks below an iPhone 6S from 2015, illustrating the gap extends across price tiers
Android-focused media largely avoids discussing the benchmark disparity, which Gruber suggests would be headline news if the situation were reversed
The unprecedented aspect isn't just Apple's chip lead but that they keep those chips exclusive rather than selling them as commodity parts
money_quotes: Apple's chip lead over Qualcomm has, if anything, widened, not narrowed.
This is a remarkable state of affairs, and a deeply inconvenient truth for Android fans (not to mention Android handset makers).
One gets the feeling that if these performance tables were turned, you'd hear a lot more about the relative benchmarks of Android vs. iOS devices from Android-focused websites than we do now.
Apple discontinued an iPhone that, if it were an Android phone, would be the fastest in single-threaded performance on the market today.
Android users who want industry-leading performance are shit out of luck. This is unprecedented in computing history.

What makes our actual situation unprecedented in personal computing history isn't that one company has maintained a decade-long CPU performance edge over the rest of the industry, but that that one company is keeping those chips exclusively for its own devices.
key_insight: Apple's exclusive, years-ahead chip advantage is historically unprecedented in personal computing — no platform leader has ever locked away the industry's best processors for its own devices alone.
